channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: From Genshin to Star Rail, HoYoFair Made It All Feel Worth Something Live
item.content:encoded:
Despite the presence of an illuminating lightning storm threatening the Dallas sky on the night of April 25th, fans still made their way out to HoYoFair at the Music Hall at Fair Park. That should tell you everything you need to know about the crowd of this year's event.

There was no mincing what this year's concert was about. Attendees showed up in full cosplay, sporting elaborate renditions of characters from Genshin Impact, Honkai: Star Rail, and Zenless Zone Zero filling the aisles before the first performer graced the stage. It was the kind of crowd that makes you realize how deeply people care about Hoyoverse, and how important finding a crowd that understands that affection really is.

It has been a while since I found myself at a concert built around the music of games I care about, and HoYoFair reminded me quickly why the presence of events like this really matter. The performances came from a lineup that pulled from both sides of the Pacific: Japanese vocalist duo Hanon and Kotoha of HaKoniwaLily, singer-songwriter majiko, utaites Amatsuki and Chogakusei, and jazz vocalist Jekaterina on one end; Broadway actor and singer Riley Costello alongside performers sirhamnet, kiamemusic, skycitizendesu, and tyih.art rounding things out on the other. The setlist moved across English, Japanese, French, and more, which is both a testament to the worldwide appeal and a performative flex as it surely transcends the limited locales that Hoyofair took place this year.

All of the music was accompanied by laser light shows and on-screen visuals pulling from each game's roster of characters, some purely illustrative, some frankly cinematic in how they wove storytelling into the performance, and it was elevated by the perfomers themselves that would often play into the on-screen antics.

I will be straight with you: I did not know every song on that setlist. Many of them I was hearing for the first time, and more than a few had me squinting at the side monitors to catch subtitles as they were sung in other languages. None of that mattered. A quick scan of the room mid-song made the case better than I could: faces lit up, lips moving in sync, people swaying in their seats with the easy comfort despite many not knowing every song on the docket either.

"The performers were great at engaging the fans and really bringing the show to life." a scantily clad anime-inspired attendee next to me said when I asked what they enjoyed most about the event. Of the several attendees around me, few considered themselves fans of the entire Hoyoverse catalog. 

And yet, those of us newer to the catalog were not left behind either. Some performances carried an emotional weight that does not require translation or prior context, with noticeable emotional reactions from those nearby with songs like Ripples of Past Reverie. On the more playful tracks, I could see the crowd mouthing along and dancing in their seats, fully in it, with a huge crowd favorite being The Heavens Full-Course.

What HoYoFair made clear is that the music inside these games is not background noise for the people who play them. It is tied to specific moments, specific characters, specific versions of themselves they return to every time they log back in. Hearing it live, surrounded by a room full of people who feel exactly the same way, is not something a playlist replicates.

The show itself, the games, and the characters was the draw. The company was the point, and I couldn't have been in better company.

Steven Weber

Steven has been a writer at MMORPG.COM since 2017. A lover of many different genres, he finds he spends most of his game time in action RPGs, and talking about himself in 3rd person on his biography page.



https://www.mmorpg.com/editorials/from-genshin-to-star-rail-hoyofair-made-it-all-feel-worth-something-live-2000137965
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
